title: Special Marks
subtitle: Marks Vim sets for you automatically.
summary: Some marks are set by Vim itself: '' for last jump, `` for last position, '. for last edit, '< / '> for last visual selection, and more.

blocks.text: Most marks you set yourself. But Vim quietly sets a handful of *special* marks all the time. They're worth memorizing — they make navigation feel telepathic.

rows: Position before the last big jump
Exact position of the last change
Line where Insert mode was last left
Exact spot Insert was last left
Start/end of last yanked or changed text

blocks.title: These Power Many Familiar Commands
blocks.text:
Special marks aren't a separate feature — they *are* the feature. Many commands you already use are implemented in terms of them:

  • {key:g}{key:v} re-selects the visual range stored in {key:'}{key:<} and {key:'}{key:>}.
  • {key:g}{key:i} re-enters Insert mode at {key:`}{key:^}.
  • {key::}{key:'}{key:<}{key:,}{key:'}{key:>} as an Ex range targets the last selection.
  • {key:`}{key:.} after a jump returns you to the last edit.

Knowing the marks gives you composability the docs don't always explain. Want to yank from the start of your last yank to here? {key:y}{key:`}{key:[}. Want to delete the most recent insertion? {key:d}{key:`}{key:[}.
{key:`}{key:`} (toggle-back-to-where-you-jumped-from) is one of the most useful marks. After almost any "big" motion ({key:G}, {key:/}, {key:%}, {key:`}{key:a}), {key:`}{key:`} reverses it.
